text: Robotic right hepatectomy with colic cystectomy for hepatocellular carcinoma.

Fifty-eight-year-old woman with a history of Crohn's disease came to the clinic with four point two centimeter mass in segment five plus eight and a second lesion in a segment seven with size of point eight centimeter
Both of them are consistent with well-differentiated HCC.
One point three centimeters is also seen.
This is the Nakamura C with a segment six pore vein branching pattern seen on the triple phase CT scan of the liver.
Patient have a normal hepatic function.
We anticipated a bit more technical challenge in the pore dissection due to the branching pattern.
The 3D model displays a larger lesion situated near the right pole pedicle, providing a visual representation of its proximity to surrounding structures.
The video shows the second lesion, which is being examined in the surgical field.
A wedge biopsy is performed on a small lesion located on the liver capsule, with visible tissue being excised for examination.
The surgeon mobilizes the liver by taking down the hepatic oval confluence, aiming to identify the notch between the right and left, as well as the right and middle hepatic veins.
The surgeon dissects and removes adhesions between the liver and diaphragm, freeing the liver from its attachment to the diaphragm.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to map out the intrahepatic vasculature, providing a detailed view of the blood vessels within the liver.
The surgeon marks the amylohepatic vein on the liver surface, noting its proximity to the medial aspect of the tumor.
The surgeon begins partial right hemoliver mobilization by detaching the posterior attachment to the Gerota's fascia.
The surgeon carefully uses a hook artery to perform a precise dissection, likely around the hepatic oval confluence area to aid in liver mobilization.
The surgeon approaches the portohepatic region, clips the cystic duct, and divides it, with visible manipulation of the duct structure.
The surgeon detaches the gallbladder from the segment five liver bed, completing its removal from the liver surface.
And this was done in standard fashion.
The surgeon clips and divides the cystic duct, completing the dissection of this structure.
The surgeon removes the prominent common bile duct lymph node as part of the lymphadenectomy, visibly excising the node from surrounding tissue.
We like to do this to obtain the lymphatic examination.
The surgeon dissects upwards to identify the pole vein branches leading to the right hemoliver, carefully navigating through the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies an accessory segment six pole inflow, highlighting its anatomical location and relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides an artery supplying segment six of the liver using scissors, visibly separating the vessel from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon isolates the segment six pole vein using a Maryland robotic instrument, clearly separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a six-inch silk suture to assist in placing hemlock clips, positioning two clips on the staying side and one on the specimen side before dividing the tissue with robotic scissors.
You can see here everything is done with precision.
The surgeon dissects and isolates the main right hepatic artery, carefully dividing surrounding lymphatic tissue using surgical instruments.
The surgeon clips and divides an accessory vessel leading to segment six of the liver using robotic instruments.
The surgeon isolates the right posterior pole vein using a robotic instrument, with the vein clearly visible in the surgical field.
As you can see on the preoperative imaging, the patient had a Nakamura type C. Therefore, the right anterior and right posterior are coming out separately from the main pole vein.
The surgeon divides the right posterior pulmonary vein, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the vein from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to identify the bile duct intrahepatically, providing visual contrast to distinguish the duct from surrounding liver tissue.
The surgeon places a bulldog clamp to test the inflow to the right hemoliver, followed by the division of the right hepatic artery between clips.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and a hokari to dissect and open access to the right anterior pole vein, following the separation of the right posterior pole vein.
The surgeon isolates the right anterior pole vein and passes a silk suture around it, securing the vein visibly within the surgical field.
The surgeon confirms the safety of the left pole vein and proceeds to mobilize the right lobe of the liver by detaching additional attachments.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral aspect of the vena cava, identifying and clipping the short hepatic veins using robotic instruments.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer on the hepatic side to manage larger short hepatic veins. Two clips are placed on these veins before division with robotic scissors, and some clips are reinforced with figure-of-eight sutures.
Traction stitches are visible on both sides of the transection plane. The inflow vessels to the right hemiliver have been ligated and divided.
The right anterior and right posterior pedicles have been divided. The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery device to transect the first centimeter of the superficial hepatic parenchyma.

The surgeon places a central line as part of the major hepatic resection protocol. As the dissection progresses deeper into the liver parenchyma, the energy device is switched to an extended vessel sealer for continued tissue division.
The surgeon identifies crossing branches intrahepatically while the assistant uses laparoscopic suction to clear smoke and irrigate the area with saline.
The surgeon has divided the liver parenchyma down to the right hepatic bile duct, which is now isolated. The bile duct is prepared for transection using a robotic stapler.
The surgeon transects the right anterior pole vein, followed by firing the stapler across the isolated right hepatic bile duct.

The liver is being opened in a book-like manner as the surgeon continues the dissection cephalad toward the notch. The right hepatic vein is stapled flush to the vena cava, with a clearly visible staple line.
The surgeon divides the remaining hepatic segment eight parenchyma with minimal bleeding. The large Makucha ligament is transected using a stapler.
The surgeon divides the remaining attachments of the coronary and triangular ligaments from the diaphragm, preparing to remove the vascularized specimen.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic approach to place the specimen into a catch bag, which is then removed through the incision at the jail point. The ICG fluorescence is visible and adequate.

The patient did very well, and the pathology showed a HCC with generous margin. Patient was discharged on post-op day six, and six-month follow-up showed no evidence of disease recurrence. Patient is doing very well without any issues.
